message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  We consider the impact of superspreading on the course of the COVID-19 epidemic. A two-component model of the epidemic has been developed, in which all infected are divided in two groups. The groups are asymptomatic
                  <jats:italic>superspreaders</jats:italic>
                  spreading the infection and
                  <jats:italic>sensitive</jats:italic>
                  persons which can only get infection. Once infected the
                  <jats:italic>sensitive</jats:italic>
                  exhibit clear symptoms and become isolated. It is shown that the ratio of increment of the number of daily cases in the beginning of the epidemic and decrement at the end of the epidemic is equal to the ratio of the spreading rates of the infection transmission from the
                  <jats:italic>superspreaders</jats:italic>
                  to potential
                  <jats:italic>superspreaders</jats:italic>
                  and to the
                  <jats:italic>sensitive</jats:italic>
                  persons, respectively. On the basis of data from 12 countries and territories it is found that the
                  <jats:italic>superspreaders</jats:italic>
                  transmit the infection to potential
                  <jats:italic>superspreaders</jats:italic>
                  approximately 4 times more often then to the
                  <jats:italic>sensitive</jats:italic>
                  persons. Specific measures to limit the epidemical incidence are proposed. The possibility of an allergic component in the disease is discussed.
                </jats:p>
title: Superspreading as a Regular Factor of the COVID-19 Pandemic: I. A Two-Component Model

reference.unstructured: Boyman O , Cervia C , Nilsson J , et al. Systemic and mucosal antibody secretion specific to SARS-CoV-2 during mild versus severe COVID-19 biorxiv 2020.05.21.108308
